item.title: Something is missing
item.content:encoded:
I understand the previous comments on this article, at the same time I understand what you wanted to do, but on the other hand, for me, it seems more like a tale then an SCP. I know there are a lot of articles that are heavy in dialog and logs (for example, SCP-5000 is just one big log), but there are at least one main idea, and we see the consequences/influences on people. But, in this case, the anomaly is too vague for me.

For this type of SCP to work, it could have more information. For example, why it is happening? How the Foundation knew about this?

I think I couldn't find the right words to express what I felt reading this. You know how to write, as shown in other articles (and in this one!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Harry's eyes well with tears.

YES.

You are my hero for the day. Thank you for noticing things :D It makes writing things worthwhile.

I didn't think anyone would get that, much less so soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just curious, but since you mentioned you link your articles together, is the alternate timeline caused (and fixed) by the events described in 5243?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Appreciate the kind words. I know where the people who don't like it are coming from; they're seeing the end-of-the-world scenarios, the people reacting, the deeper implications, and imagining the stories those elements could support, and not seeing any of that reflected here. But yes, really I was just trying for enough of a framework to support that sudden jolt, and the fridge horror. That way this file is really just the end point of any timeline-destroying narratives you can think of; it's actually the end point of a few timeline-destroying narratives I'm eventually going to post as tales. I didn't think it needed its own dedicated, specific narrative to work, and for two thirds of the readers, it looks like it doesn't. About what I expected.

I agree with the thing about the discussion page; I just did another skip with researcher annotations, SCP-5866, which handles the drafting process very differently. I always intended to do it that way, because that one actually has a narrative in the annotations. This one, I wanted to keep them out of the way for visual clarity.

I'm very glad it worked for you in each case, and thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're getting a lot of negative comments about this, but I think it's pretty damn near perfect. Using the footnotes for the back and forth between the characters was jarring at first but by the 4th annotation it was an easy switch. I think if the Foundation was real they'd use something like a discussion page for those sorts of conversations but this was a wonderful way to include it as part of the skip.

And that ending. I thought it achieved exactly what you aimed for.

Easiest +1 I've ever had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that was unexpected! It's like I gave you a list of the notes I was hoping to hit, and you wrote a post saying I hit them.

+ Clearance Level -1 Instructions
- For general dissemination

While doing research for a tale series, I noticed that LurkD has a tendency to do sequential collapsibles with security clearance levels; not something I was thinking about while doing this, but you reminded me of it and yeah, I think that kind of thing is neat.

You're right about the characters, they're really just there to enable the narrative — enough of a human touch that we understand why the note at the bottom was erroneously left in, so that we understand what's going on when we see the note again in the final iteration. Hopefully enough that we get a sense of a real world about to end. I was hoping for a little poignancy, but didn't figure I'd get a lot. I'm glad there was enough there for you.

I haven't gotten around to reading Arbelict's Proposal yet, but at a glance I can see what you mean.

I liked the idea that the first few levels of clearance were nested layers of bullshit, so that everyone but O5 was getting a sanitized version of the truth. A whole spectrum of sanitization for the end of the world.

I agree that this is the kind of scenario that happens all the time, as time travel is a popular topic, but we never really think about what happens to the versions of ourselves who get offed when we set right what once went wrong. I have a time loop SCP of my own, and this one is a direct response to its implications.

For me, the reader's universe being erased while the page refreshes was the vital component of the whole thing. That moment was what I wrote everything else to support, and I'm very glad it works for you.

(I've been going through the most popular classes, using them as inspiration one by one, and the thing I hate about Apollyon [beyond the fact that it's edgelordy] is that it's universe-ending stuff by nature, and my stories are all interconnected, and I'm not looking to end that universe any time soon. This was my solution!)

Thanks for catching the Containment Class mistake! And here I thought I'd combed over every word enough times already. Nope. Fixt!

Anyway yes, I really, really appreciate this comment. Not that I don't appreciate the comments from people who wanted more and didn't get it, but it's great to know that the target audience found something effective here. Thank you very much indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I only just remembered I had taken some notes on what I enjoyed of this when I read it last week. This post isn't so much a critique as a deconstruction. Anyway:

+ Thoughts
- Thoughts

Special Containment Procedures

First, this article isn't some one-off anomaly — in-universe, it has weight.

We see here the first escalation, and it's immediate. I like that this provides concentric shells of secrecy according to clearance. This is something I've come to expect in SCP documents, but rarely ever see.

There is something bittersweet about the notes from the presiding archivists. They aren't serving anything heartfelt or dramatic. And while I'm not expecting some award-winning performance from one-line notes in a short article — I don't think that's the point. These characters are our POV into the scenario, and they provide, simply, a layer of added depth. Without them, we have a standard Lily's Proposal-esque presentation1 —

When SCP-5618 is imminent, the O5 Council must review the following instructions and arrange for their dissemination.

— but with them, we are afforded sprinklings of personality. And I appreciate it.

All Security Clearance Level 0 personnel are to be immediately terminated.

Similar vibes as in Arbelict's Proposal. Purely a coincidence, I'm sure, but another thing I enjoy.

Evacuation to persistent alternate dimensions and timelines is barred by the Multi-Foundation Pact of 1981, as is the transfer of goods or information.

In the containment alone we navigate a full spectrum of expectations. And that's the dead end, all right. Gut punch.

Description

SCP-5618 is the erasure of the alternate timeline we inhabit, following a successful attempt to restore baseline reality via historical intervention.

I honestly really enjoy this because, despite erring on the shorter side, it provides greater insight into something which might be considered "universal canon". It asks, what happens in an alt universe that no longer matters? Once this Foundation has fixed their problem, somewhere, and ensured life, somewhere — then what?

Well, maybe in the grand scheme of things, they don't matter. But they certainly do to themselves, for what that's worth.

There is canonical value here. Maybe the telling of this story is not necessarily important, but damn if it doesn't fill a niche.

RAISA Notice

This file is undergoing revision to reflect changing circumstances.

They refresh a page, and cease to exist. By clicking the link, we're starting the cycle anew, and doing away with the old. For good.

New Iteration

1. I hope we never have to fill this thing out. [SZ]
2. If we do, it won't really be us. Not for long, anyway. [AF]

These are the closing lines of the article. It's small, but I think there's a lot to enjoy here. This is as good a way as any to end it, to make it feel like it's finished, and personally, I find it effective.

Footnotes
1. Not something particularly impressive, if exceedingly successful.
+ Nitpick(s)
- Nitpick(s)

The Object Class, Disruption Class, Risk Class, Special Containment Procedures and Description are blank because the intended subject is theoretical.

Because you use the ACS, this Notice (in both offsets) should refer to Containment Class instead of Object Class.

I enjoyed this and thought it was worth more than a simple "I enjoyed this". Hope this post is useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's the goal! Thanks.

It's hard not to make more 'blank' jokes than I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I certainly wouldn't want to live in one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short n' sweet. +1.

Aside: it's great how the 'punchline' is a blank, written by HarryBlank. Very on brand. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The timelines where this has not been upvoted by me are not worth saving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! That's all I was going for, this time around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice “holy shi— that’s it?!” At the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's more that the piece wants to be about a gut reaction, but also wants to make enough of a gesture to a story that it's not entirely barren. Frankly the contents of the piece are just "here's the basic, pared-down list of things the Foundation would do differently in this situation," to shuffle the reader along to the point where their existence is terminated between pages loading. I'm sorry it didn't work for you, but it's pretty much working as intended I think. If it ends up lasting, that's cool, if it crashes and burns, that just means one of the two reactions I figured it would get turns out to represent more people than the other. Which will be educational for me at least :P

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Glad it worked for you! Particularly glad the sudden starkness worked for you, that was the whole ballgame for me.

item.title: Re: YES, MORE APOLLYON >:D
item.content:encoded:
I thought about doing in-text annotations but I couldn't figure out how to make it not be painful to look at, that way. I've got ideas for something like that in the future.

Thanks for the vote, and the comment :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could have been expanded considerably, but I made the conscious decision not to. Some people will be jolted by the abrupt ending, which is my intention; some people will go "but what else?" which is not what I want but I knew it would happen. I gambled that there'd be more of the former than the latter, and so far, there has been… if not necessarily by a lot :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the premise could certainly support a detailed story. Normally that's what I'd do, everything I write tries to completely plumb the concept for what it's worth. I was intentionally not doing that here, going for something fast and surprising; I knew ahead of time that it would get a lot of reactions like yours, and I wasn't expecting otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The blank file is the essence of the quick reaction I was hoping for; it works for some people, it doesn't work for some other people. I'm cool with that, it was expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *shrugs*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't make ACS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this piece couldn't decide between being about the characters, or about the foundation responding to a temporal collapse, and rather than take the best of both worlds ended up in a space where neither really shined.

The levity of the footnotes takes away from the gravity of the situation rather than emphasizing it. Like, I can appreciate that sort of "well, it's the end of the world I guess" tone, but it doesn't work well in contrast to a very rigid clinical tone. If you wanted this to be a piece that focusses more on the characters, I would want to give them more space to breathe and develop so we can feel actual attachment to them.

If this piece was more supposed to be about the foundation's reaction as a whole, I would do away with the footnotes and make the conprocs tell a more detailed and nuanced story of what the foundation considers valuable. Right now the conprocs feel a little bland in terms of "things the foundation would do". It leaves a lot up to generalization and abstraction rather than zeroing on particularly evocative details.

I also really don't think the listpages was effective here. Like, you could've just let the article end on the first page and I think you would've gotten the point across. The refresh button and next page artificially lengthens the piece. While sure, the piece isn't that long to begin with, that does make any additional length much more noticeable. If you're going for short, you really need to make sure you're making every word count, and most of the words on the new page are just a rehash of a memo on the previous page.

Overall, I think if this wants to be about the end of the world, it would be better to decide what part of the end of the world you want to actually focus on.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I like the general idea, the execution leaves quite a bit to be desired. I feel like it’s going for fridge horror, but the ending needs to be a lot more satisfying for that to take effect. In addition, "Level 6 / Cosmic Top Secret" doesn’t really make sense.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's so many footnotes that don't seem actually important and it just makes it hard to read I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first iteration of this page acts as some effective buildup, and although the characters are a bit bland, it prepares you for the expectation that you might get to know them better, and really come to care about them and their place within this story. Unfortunately, the second iteration doesn't do very much with all that buildup and is essentially a blank file. I really wish I could like this, but there's so little beneath the surface that it just ends up feeling a bit bland and shallow. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article isn’t technically bad, but it’s bland and that might be worse. SZ and AF are barely characters; their dialogue is spartan and only tells us that they’ve worked together before. Both the disaster and the buildup feel underwhelming because the CK class shift is never expounded upon and the containment procedures don’t do a good job of demonstrating the escalating scale of the threat. The ending is similarly predictable — the timeline was wiped out but I don’t feel any sense of loss because I barely knew it. There’s a lot of similarities here at the idea level to SCP-2317 and Lily’s Proposal, but the former actually escalates the threat and thus intrigue of the anomaly and the latter actively creates a melancholy tone that sells the poignancy of its core idea. 5618, in comparison, lays out a threat but fails to make the escalation feel meaningful or convey the poignancy of the end of the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a moment just before you click 'refresh file' where you're hyped for more, for further exploration of the concept. Instead, we're greeted with an effectively blank file.

The first iteration serves as a really nice like…introduction to a story; but I can't help but feel like an exploration more akin to SCP-5552 in style would've had left this feeling stronger; where we see how the characters react in different time lines; or where there's more exploration of e.g. the multi-foundation pact or just…something more than what we have here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, the overall narrative is…okay? But, I think it should've been expanded upon more. In the end, as much as I find the idea interesting, how it was executed made this underwhelming and ended up being unsatisfying and lacking.
-1

item.title: YES, MORE APOLLYON >:D
item.content:encoded:
The footnotes as edit notes was a bit jarring—maybe I would have tried to use a new system that hadn't had its purpose reinforced with every other article on the site—but it was short enough for me to re-read and re-orient myself. Not that I'm clever enough to write this anyway.

But it was a good little two reads. Have an upvote.

I really enjoyed this.

Whilst I expected the offset page to reflect the alternate timeline's erasure, I didn't expect it to be completely bare, bar the DoTA note. The footnotes were a great method of injecting characters into the story without minimising the evocativeness of the article's core idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks! You have executed said duty excellent well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why what a marvelous little story you have spun here, Harry! I say, it is my wish, no, my duty!, to upvote it.

item.title: Holy shit that was hard
item.content:encoded:
Fuckin' listpages, man.

I wanted to tell a little story, but get you to tell a little story with your mouse click, too. Did it work?

Thanks to Grigori Karpin, Placeholder McD and Calibri Bold for looking this over.

The departmental logo is a bigger version, with transparency, of the one from Document 1780-WL written by HammerMaiden. This recreation was made by me.
